questions.question: Define encapsulation and explain how granting access helps in achieving encapsulation.
keyPoints: Encapsulation is bundling data and methods into a single unit
It restricts direct access to data members
Controlled access through public member functions (getters/setters)
Friend functions provide selective access to private members while maintaining encapsulation
Access specifiers enforce data hiding

questions.question: What is a friend function? Explain with a suitable example.
keyPoints: Friend function is a non-member function with special privileges
Declared using 'friend' keyword inside class
Can access private and protected members
Example: function outside class declared as friend
Useful for operator overloading and inter-class operations

questions.question: Differentiate between private and protected access specifiers in C++.
keyPoints: Private: accessible only within declaring class
Protected: accessible within class and derived classes
Derived class cannot access private base members
Protected allows inheritance while maintaining secrecy from outside
Protected provides controlled inheritance access

questions.question: Explain the 'using' declaration in C++ with an example.
keyPoints: Using declaration introduces base class name into derived class scope
Syntax: using BaseClass::memberName;
Allows derived class to access protected/private members of base
Used to selectively grant access to inherited members
Helps resolve name ambiguities in multiple inheritance

questions.question: What is a friend class? When and why is it used?
keyPoints: Friend class grants all its member functions access to another class's private members
Declared as 'friend class ClassName;'
Used when two classes need close cooperation
Allows sharing of private data between related classes
Should be used sparingly to avoid breaking encapsulation

questions.question: State and explain any three properties of friendship in C++.
keyPoints: 1. Friendship is not transitive - A friend of B is not friend of C
2. Friendship is not inherited - derived classes don't inherit friendship
3. Friendship is not commutative - must be explicitly granted
4. Friendship is not bidirectional unless explicitly stated
5. Friend functions are non-members but have same access as member functions

questions.question: Explain the concept of granting access in C++ OOP. Discuss all access specifiers with examples and demonstrate how they control member accessibility.
keyPoints: Introduction to access control in OOP
Public access specifier - accessible everywhere
Private access specifier - accessible only within class
Protected access specifier - accessible in class and derived classes
Default access for classes is private, for structs is public
Example program demonstrating all specifiers
Role of access specifiers in encapsulation
Friend functions as mechanism to selectively grant access

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate the use of friend functions and friend classes. Also explain the differences between them.
keyPoints: Definition and declaration of friend function
Program with at least one friend function
Definition and declaration of friend class
Program demonstrating friend class
Friend function: non-member with special access
Friend class: all members get special access
Differences in scope and usage
When to use each approach

questions.question: Explain the concept of encapsulation in detail. How does C++ implement encapsulation? How does granting access support encapsulation?
keyPoints: Definition of encapsulation
Data bundling and data hiding aspects
Access specifiers for implementing encapsulation
Public interface for controlled access
Private data with public methods
Friend functions: selective access granting
Using declarations for controlled inheritance access
Benefits of encapsulation - security, modularity, maintainability

questions.question: Discuss in detail about various mechanisms for granting access in C++. Include friend functions, friend classes, and using declarations with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Access specifiers as primary mechanism
Friend functions for non-member access
Friend classes for inter-class access
Using declarations for base class member access
Static members and their access patterns
Nested classes and friendship
Properties and limitations of each mechanism
Best practices for controlled access
